intro:
Israel is the covenant name given to the patriarch Jacob after his night of wrestling with a divine adversary at the ford of the Jabbok: Genesis 32:28 — "Your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel, for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed." The Hebrew yisra'el joins the verb sarah (to strive, contend, or rule) with 'El (God), and is read in the text as he who strives with God. The new name was solemnly reaffirmed at Bethel in Genesis 35:10. From this single man the name expanded outward: first to his household, the "sons of Israel"; then to the twelve tribes descended from his sons (see Genesis 49:28); and finally to the nation, the elect people of God among whom He chose to dwell.

The biblical-theology arc of Israel is the spine of the Old Testament. The people are forged in the furnace of Egyptian bondage and brought out by the mighty hand of God in the Exodus (Exodus 4:22, where God calls Israel "my firstborn son"). At Sinai they are constituted a covenant nation, summoned to be "a kingdom of priests and a holy nation" (Exodus 19:6) and bound to the LORD by the Law given through Moses. After the wilderness years they enter and possess Canaan under Joshua. Israel is governed by judges, then asks for a king; the monarchy rises under Saul, David, and Solomon, reaching its zenith with the temple in Jerusalem. At Solomon's death the kingdom splits (1 Kings 12:16): the ten northern tribes retain the name Israel, while the south becomes Judah. The north, mired in idolatry, falls to Assyria in 722 BC (2 Kings 17:6); Judah is carried to Babylon in 586 BC. Throughout, the prophets use "Israel" with shifting reference — the northern kingdom, the whole covenant people, and the purified remnant of future promise (Isaiah 10:20–22, Hosea 11:1) — and they foretell a new covenant written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:31–34).

Israel's election was never for privilege alone but for the blessing of all nations, fulfilling the promise to Abraham that in his seed "all the families of the earth shall be blessed" (Genesis 12:3). The New Testament reads this story as reaching its goal in Jesus, the true and faithful Israelite who, called out of Egypt (Matthew 2:15), succeeds where the nation failed. Paul insists that physical descent does not by itself define the people of God — "For not all who are descended from Israel belong to Israel" (Romans 9:6) — yet he equally affirms that God has not cast off His ancient people and that "all Israel will be saved" (Romans 11:26). The church, grafted in among the natural branches (Romans 11:17), can be called "the Israel of God" (Galatians 6:16). The name thus carries both historical particularity — a real people with a real land and history — and eschatological breadth, gathering Jew and Gentile into the one people of the covenant, and looking forward to the consummation pictured in the twelve tribes of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:12).
